items.prompt: What does lower_bound(target) return in ascending data?

options.text: The first index whose value is greater than or equal to target.
The first index whose value is strictly greater than target.
The last index whose value is less than or equal to target.
Any index equal to target.

feedback.reason: Lower bound locates the insertion boundary before all values that are not less than target.
feedback.details: It returns n when every value is smaller than target.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.last_le: That can be derived from upper_bound but is not lower_bound.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.any_equal: Lower bound remains defined even when target is absent.

items.prompt: What does upper_bound(target) return in ascending data?

options.text: The first index whose value is strictly greater than target.
The first index whose value is greater than or equal to target.
The last index equal to target.
Any index whose value is greater than target.

feedback.reason: Upper bound separates values less than or equal to target from strictly greater values.
feedback.details: It can equal n when no value is greater.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.last_equal: Upper bound is one position after the final equal value.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.any_greater: The first such index is required.

items.prompt: The sorted array is [1, 2, 2, 2, 5]. What are lower_bound(2) and upper_bound(2)?

options.text: 1 and 4.
1 and 3.
2 and 4.
0 and 5.

feedback.reason: The equal-value range is the half-open interval [lower_bound, upper_bound).
feedback.details: Indices 1, 2, and 3 contain 2, so the first greater value is at index 4.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.one_three: Index 3 is the last equal value, not the first greater value.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.two_four: Lower bound must return the first equal position.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.zero_five: Those are the full-array boundaries, not the target boundaries.

items.prompt: How can the number of occurrences of target in sorted data be computed from boundaries?

options.text: upper_bound(target) - lower_bound(target).
lower_bound(target) - upper_bound(target).
upper_bound(target) + lower_bound(target).
Return 1 whenever any exact match exists.

feedback.reason: The half-open equal range contains exactly upper - lower indices.
feedback.details: When target is absent, both boundaries converge to the same insertion position and the count is zero.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.upper_plus_lower: Index positions are not combined by addition.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.any_match: Duplicates may produce more than one occurrence.

items.prompt: What should lower_bound(target) return when every array value is smaller than target?

options.text: n, the insertion position after the final element.
n - 1, the final valid index.

feedback.reason: The insertion boundary may be one past the final valid index.
feedback.details: Half-open boundary searches intentionally allow n as a legal result.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.n_minus_one: That value is still smaller than target.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.minus_one: Lower bound commonly uses the half-open sentinel n.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.zero: Inserting at the beginning would violate sorted order.

items.prompt: What should lower_bound(target) return when every array value is greater than target?

options.text: n.

feedback.reason: The first position already satisfies the lower-bound predicate.
feedback.details: Inserting target before index 0 preserves ascending order.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.one: The first element already satisfies value >= target.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.n_minus_one: That is not the first valid position.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.n: A valid position exists at the beginning.

items.prompt: Why is returning immediately when nums[mid] equals target incorrect for lower_bound?

options.text: An earlier equal value may still exist in the left region.
Equality is never a valid lower-bound condition.
Lower bound always lies to the right of an equal value.
Duplicates make the array unsorted.

feedback.reason: A matching midpoint is a valid candidate but may not be the first valid candidate.
feedback.details: The search retains mid and continues left.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.must_search_right: It seeks the first equal or greater position.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.duplicates_unsorted: Equal adjacent values are compatible with sorted order.

items.prompt: How can the last index with value <= target be derived from upper_bound?

options.text: upper_bound(target) - 1, when the result range is non-empty.
lower_bound(target) + 1.
upper_bound(target).
Always return n.

feedback.reason: Upper bound is the first position outside the <= target region.
feedback.details: Subtracting one yields the final position inside that region, with a separate check when upper bound is zero.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.lower_plus_one: This has no general relation to the last <= target position.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.upper_itself: Upper bound points to the first strictly greater value.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.n_always: The result depends on target and data.

items.prompt: How can the last index with value < target be derived from lower_bound?

options.text: lower_bound(target) - 1, when lower_bound is greater than zero.
upper_bound(target) - 1.
lower_bound(target).
Always return 0.

feedback.reason: Lower bound is the first position outside the < target region.
feedback.details: The predecessor is the final strictly smaller value when such a position exists.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.upper_minus_one: That returns the last <= target position.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.lower_itself: Lower bound points to the first >= target position.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.zero_always: The answer may not exist or may be later.

items.prompt: The target is absent from sorted data. How are lower_bound(target) and upper_bound(target) related?

options.text: They are equal at the target's insertion position.
Lower bound is always strictly smaller.
Upper bound is smaller than lower bound.
Both must be -1.

feedback.reason: With no values equal to target, the >= and > predicates change at the same position.
feedback.details: This makes upper - lower equal zero.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.lower_less: Without equal values, first >= and first > coincide.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.both_minus_one: The canonical boundary result is an insertion index, possibly n.

items.prompt: Which are legal lower-bound return values for an array of length n? Select all that apply.

options.text: Any index from 1 through n - 1.
n.
-1 under the canonical [0, n) contract.

feedback.reason: Lower bound returns an insertion boundary in the closed numeric range [0, n].
feedback.details: It is not limited to valid element indices because n is a useful sentinel.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.minus_one: The canonical insertion boundary stays within 0 through n.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.zero: The first element may satisfy the predicate.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.middle: The transition may occur inside the array.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.n: No element may satisfy value >= target.

items.prompt: Which queries can be answered using lower or upper boundaries? Select all that apply.

options.text: Find where target can be inserted while preserving order.
Count occurrences of target.
Find the last value less than or equal to target.
Find any target in arbitrary unsorted data without preprocessing.

feedback.reason: Boundary searches expose ordered partition points that support several derived queries.
feedback.details: They do not apply directly to arbitrary unsorted data.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.arbitrary_unsorted: Boundary predicates rely on sorted or monotonic structure.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.insertion: Lower bound is the canonical insertion point before equal values.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.count_equal: The equal range length is upper - lower.

items.prompt: Which tests are important for lower and upper bound implementations? Select all that apply.

options.text: Target absent between two existing values.
All array values equal target.
Target smaller than all values and larger than all values.
Only one unique target located initially at mid.

feedback.reason: Boundary tests must exercise duplicate ranges, absence, and both sentinels.
feedback.details: A midpoint hit can pass even when the algorithm returns the wrong edge.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.only_unique_mid: This does not exercise duplicate or sentinel behavior.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.target_absent_between: Both boundaries should equal the internal insertion point.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.all_equal: The boundaries should become 0 and n.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.outside_both: These exercise return values 0 and n.

items.prompt: Which predicate-to-boundary mappings are correct? Select all that apply.

options.text: First value >= target corresponds to lower_bound.
First value > target corresponds to upper_bound.
Last value < target can be derived as lower_bound(target) - 1.
Last value <= target is lower_bound(target) - 1.

feedback.reason: Boundary definitions can be composed through predecessor relationships.
feedback.details: The strictness of the predicate determines whether lower or upper bound is used.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.last_le_lower: That gives last < target, not last <= target when equal values exist.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.first_ge: This is the canonical lower-bound predicate.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.first_gt: This is the canonical upper-bound predicate.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.last_lt: Lower bound is the first position outside the < target region.

items.prompt: Arrange one lower-bound iteration over [left, right).

elements.text: Compute a midpoint in the non-empty half-open interval.
If true set right = mid; otherwise set left = mid + 1.

feedback.reason: The predicate is false before the lower bound and true from the lower bound onward.
feedback.details: A true midpoint remains a candidate; a false midpoint is discarded.

items.prompt: Arrange one upper-bound iteration over [left, right).

elements.text: If true set right = mid; otherwise set left = mid + 1.

feedback.reason: Upper bound searches the first true position of value > target.
feedback.details: Equal values belong to the false side and are skipped by moving left past mid.

items.prompt: Arrange the steps for counting target occurrences in sorted data.

elements.text: Treat zero as target absent and a positive value as the count.

feedback.reason: The equal-value region is half-open and its length is a direct subtraction.
feedback.details: No separate exact-match scan is required.

items.prompt: Arrange the verification of a returned lower-bound index p.

elements.text: Verify every index before p has value < target.
Verify p == n or nums[p] >= target.
Conclude p is the first valid insertion boundary.

feedback.reason: A boundary result is proven by the partition on both sides.
feedback.details: Exact equality is optional because target may be absent.

items.prompt: Two iterative boundary searches compute lower_bound and upper_bound on n sorted values. Select total time and auxiliary-space complexity.
constraints: Each search performs O(1) work per iteration.

feedback.reason: Two logarithmic searches still sum to O(log n).
feedback.details: Each uses only boundary and midpoint variables.

items.prompt: An unsorted array of n values is sorted once, then q lower-bound queries are answered. Select total time and additional space assuming an in-place O(n log n) sort.
constraints: Each query uses iterative binary search.
The input may be reordered by the contract.

time: O(n log n + q log n)
O(n log n + q log n)

time.sort_plus_queries: O(n log n + q log n)

feedback.reason: The preprocessing cost and all query costs must both be included.
feedback.details: This tradeoff is attractive only when reordering is legal and query volume justifies sorting.
